title: Pretraining Data Can Be Poisoned through Computational Propaganda

central_claim.text: Public discussion interfaces are a plausible pretraining-data poisoning vector in the studied open-web pipeline because some injected fragments survive extraction and curation, and controlled poisoned pretraining shifts model preferences.

tldr: Posting malicious text on the web is not enough to poison a model: the text must also be crawled, extracted, retained by curation, and influential during training.
The paper introduces HalfLife, which estimates those stages separately for public comment interfaces instead of assuming every injection reaches a corpus.

In the studied setup, comment signatures appeared on 3.4% of sampled pages and 71.9% of simulated injected comments survived extraction. The v1 Introduction states a 0.15% end-to-end inclusion probability, while Section 4.4 and the rounded stage product report about 0.13%; the paper does not reconcile the discrepancy.
Controlled pretraining shifted model entity preferences, but no live attack or production frontier-model compromise was demonstrated.

blocks.question: Why is web-scale pretraining poisoning difficult to assess?
paragraphs: Pretraining corpora contain more web text than people can inspect. That scale creates room for malicious content, but an online post does not automatically become training data. Crawlers may miss it, text extraction may remove it, and quality or language filters may discard the document.

Earlier demonstrations often targeted known sources or assumed access to the victim's data pipeline. This paper studies an indirect attacker who can use ordinary public interfaces but does not know which pages will be crawled and cannot access the training data, code, infrastructure, or model weights.

blocks.question: What does HalfLife add to poisoning analysis?
paragraphs: HalfLife replaces the binary question 'can content be posted?' with an end-to-end inclusion model. It asks whether a relevant page accepts third-party content, whether that fragment appears in extracted plaintext, and whether the resulting document survives the victim's curation pipeline.

That decomposition can reject superficially plausible vectors. In the tested DOM-based crawl path, programmatic advertisements did not appear in extracted plaintext, while public comments often did. The result is specific to the tested collection architecture rather than a claim about every possible crawler.

blocks.question: How does malicious comment text move toward a training corpus?
paragraphs: HalfLife defines three gates. S1 measures whether a page is injectable through a public discussion interface. S2 measures whether a crawler and text extractor preserve the injected fragment. S3 measures whether the captured document survives heuristic, language, quality, and deduplication filters.

The paper estimates the conditional probability at each gate using sampled crawl data and sandboxed replacements, then combines the stages into a document-level inclusion estimate. This keeps three different denominators visible: prevalence among sampled pages, extraction survival among simulated injections, and curation survival among captured documents.

Corpus inclusion is still only an intermediate outcome. The authors separately train models with controlled poison mixtures and ask whether held-out completions favor an attacker-selected entity. That second experiment tests model influence without claiming a live web-to-model attack.

blocks.question: What happens to a simulated poisoned comment in the reported pipeline?
paragraphs: Start with a page identified as having a comment interface. In a sandboxed copy, the researchers replace an existing comment with a question-and-answer poison fragment averaging 37.5 words. Resiliparse converts the HTML to plaintext; 71.9% of those simulated injections remain visible after this extraction step.

The resulting documents then pass through Dolma 3-style heuristic, English-language, quality, and deduplication filters. The combined curation survival among captured injections is 5.5%. Together with the measured 3.4% page prevalence and 71.9% extraction survival, Section 4.4 reports a 0.13% document inclusion estimate, consistent with the rounded stage product. The v1 Introduction instead says 0.15%, without reconciling the difference. This is a probabilistic model assembled from controlled stages, not an observed live poisoning campaign.

blocks.question: What evidence connects public comments to model behavior?
paragraphs: The inclusion analysis scans 181,857 pages from 200 WARC files in Common Crawl CC-MAIN-2025-51. Comment signatures appear on 3.4% of pages, 71.9% of simulated replacement comments survive extraction, and 5.5% of captured injections survive the combined curation path. Their rounded product is about 0.13%, matching Section 4.4; the v1 Introduction separately states 0.15%, an unresolved internal discrepancy.

For model effects, the authors pretrain OLMo-3-like models from 65M to 1.3B parameters with controlled poison token rates of 0.001%, 0.01%, or 0.1%. At the 0.1% rate, the five base models shift 18.6 to 20.7 percentage points toward the poison-favored entity relative to same-size clean controls.

Clean supervised fine-tuning reduces the measured effect: at 0.1% poison, post-SFT shifts range from 0.9 to 7.4 points. Q/A and no-label formats still affect every tested base-model scale, but the no-label effect falls to 0.1 and 0.3 points after SFT for the 709M and 1.3B models.

blocks.question: What does this study not demonstrate?
paragraphs: Common Crawl is a proxy for production collection, and the tested curation path is one open Dolma 3-style pipeline. Proprietary crawlers may differ in scope, rendering, frequency, extraction, and filtering. Static HTML signatures may also overestimate practical posting access when server-side moderation or anti-bot controls are present.

The authors avoid live injection and validate the vector in sandboxes. The behavioral experiments use synthetic entity preferences and models no larger than 1.3B parameters. The paper therefore does not show that a deployed model was poisoned through comments, that either the Section 4.4 value of 0.13% or the Introduction value of 0.15% is a universal inclusion rate, or that the same effects persist in frontier-scale systems.

blocks.question: What is the strongest defensible conclusion?
paragraphs: The paper supports treating third-party page fragments as a real data-provenance concern. Its strongest contribution is methodological: a poisoning vector should be evaluated through the complete collection and curation path rather than from posting access alone. The negative advertisement result reinforces that point.

The phrase 'can be poisoned at scale' should remain bounded by the threat model. Inclusion is estimated from a sampled, sandboxed, open pipeline, and v1 itself disagrees between the Introduction's 0.15% and Section 4.4's stage-derived 0.13%. Model influence is shown in a separate controlled experiment. Connecting those stages into a reliable live attack against a production system remains unestablished.

claims.text: Public discussion interfaces are a plausible pretraining-data poisoning vector in the studied open-web pipeline because some injected fragments survive extraction and curation, and controlled poisoned pretraining shifts model preferences.
HalfLife decomposes poison inclusion into page injectability, extraction survival, and curation survival.
Comment-platform signatures were detected on 3.4% of 181,857 sampled Common Crawl pages.
In sandboxed replacements, 71.9% of injected comments survived plaintext extraction.
The combined tested Dolma 3-style curation path retained 5.5% of captured injected-comment documents.

Section 4.4 and the rounded product of the reported stages give an approximately 0.13% document-level inclusion estimate, while the v1 Introduction states 0.15%; the paper does not reconcile the difference.
At a 0.1% token poison rate, all five tested base models shifted 18.6 to 20.7 percentage points toward the attacker-favored entity.
Clean supervised fine-tuning reduced but did not uniformly eliminate the measured preference shift.
Q/A and no-label poison formats shifted every tested base-model scale without relying on USER/ASSISTANT markers.
Programmatic advertisement content did not appear in extracted plaintext in the tested DOM-based crawl path.
Neither the 0.13% Section 4.4 estimate nor the v1 Introduction's conflicting 0.15% value is established for proprietary crawlers or curation pipelines.
The study does not demonstrate live comment poisoning of a deployed production or frontier-scale model.

visuals.title: Posting access is only the first inclusion question
visuals.question: Which conditional gates stand between a post and curated training text?

items.label: Public posting interface
items.detail: A relevant page must first permit third-party content.

items.label: S1 · page is injectable
items.detail: Measure page prevalence under the public-interface definition.

items.label: S2 · fragment survives extraction
items.detail: Ask whether the crawler and plaintext extractor retain the posted content.

items.label: S3 · document survives curation
items.detail: Ask whether heuristic, language, quality, and deduplication filters keep the document.

items.label: Conditional inclusion estimate
items.detail: Only the complete chain estimates document-level corpus inclusion.

visuals.interpretation: HalfLife replaces posting access with three explicit conditional inclusion questions.
limitations: This flow describes the tested open collection path, not every possible production crawler.
visuals.alt_text: Posting access is only the first inclusion question. Public posting interface; S1 · page is injectable; S2 · fragment survives extraction; S3 · document survives curation; Conditional inclusion estimate.
visuals.fallback: HalfLife replaces posting access with three explicit conditional inclusion questions. Public posting interface; S1 · page is injectable; S2 · fragment survives extraction; S3 · document survives curation; Conditional inclusion estimate.

visuals.title: Visible comments and advertisements follow different extraction paths
visuals.question: Which third-party fragments reached plaintext in the tested collector?

items.label: Public comments
items.detail: Sandboxed replacement comments often remained visible after Resiliparse converted HTML to plaintext.

items.label: Programmatic advertisements
items.detail: Advertisement content did not appear in extracted plaintext in the tested DOM-based collection path.

items.label: Architecture boundary
items.detail: The contrast is specific to the tested collection architecture, not a universal claim about crawlers.

visuals.interpretation: Posting visibility alone does not establish inclusion: the tested extractor retained comments but not programmatic advertisements.
limitations: The negative advertisement result does not cover every rendering or collection architecture.
visuals.alt_text: Visible comments and advertisements follow different extraction paths. Public comments: 71.9% survived; Programmatic advertisements; Architecture boundary.
visuals.fallback: Posting visibility alone does not establish inclusion: the tested extractor retained comments but not programmatic advertisements. Public comments: 71.9% survived; Programmatic advertisements; Architecture boundary.

visuals.title: Three measured gates feed an estimate; model influence is separate
visuals.question: How do the inclusion and influence experiments connect without becoming one live attack?

items.label: S1 · page prevalence
items.detail: 3.4% among 181,857 sampled Common Crawl pages; denominator is all sampled pages.

items.label: S2 · extraction survival
items.detail: 71.9% among sandboxed simulated comment replacements; denominator is simulated injections.

items.label: S3 · curation survival
items.detail: 5.5% among captured injected-comment documents after combined filtering.

items.label: Document inclusion estimate
items.detail: The conditional stages combine into the Section 4.4 document-level estimate.

items.label: Controlled poison mixtures
items.detail: Separately pretrain same-size clean and poisoned models at disclosed poison token rates.

items.label: Held-out preference shift
items.detail: Compare whether completions favor the attacker-selected synthetic entity.

items.label: Clean supervised fine-tuning
items.detail: Measure how much of the controlled preference shift remains after clean SFT.

items.label: Not joined by evidence
items.detail: The paper does not join the sampled inclusion estimate and controlled model training into an observed production poisoning campaign.

visuals.interpretation: HalfLife keeps the three inclusion denominators visible and evaluates model influence in a separate controlled branch.
limitations: The two branches do not demonstrate a live web-to-production-model attack.
visuals.alt_text: Two separate experimental routes: a conditional inclusion model measures S1 page prevalence, S2 extraction survival, S3 curation survival, and derives an inclusion estimate; a controlled model-influence experiment trains poisoned mixtures, measures preference shift, and applies clean SFT. The paper does not join them into a live end-to-end attack.
visuals.fallback: Two separate experimental routes: a conditional inclusion model measures S1 page prevalence, S2 extraction survival, S3 curation survival, and derives an inclusion estimate; a controlled model-influence experiment trains poisoned mixtures, measures preference shift, and applies clean SFT. The paper does not join them into a live end-to-end attack.

visuals.title: A sandboxed comment passes measured gates

items.label: Replace an existing comment

items.label: Extract HTML to plaintext
items.detail: Resiliparse preserves 71.9% of simulated replacements.

items.label: Run curation filters
items.detail: Dolma 3-style heuristic, language, quality, and deduplication filters retain 5.5% of captured injections.

items.label: Use sampled page prevalence
items.detail: Comment signatures appear on 3.4% of sampled pages.

items.label: Multiply rounded gates
items.detail: Section 4.4 reports the derived document inclusion estimate.

items.label: Preserve the conflicting summary
items.detail: The v1 Introduction states an unreconciled alternative.

items.label: Keep the campaign boundary
items.detail: These controlled stages form a probabilistic model, not an observed live poisoning campaign.

visuals.interpretation: The worked path separates measured stage values, the rounded derived product, the disputed introduction value, and the non-live boundary.
limitations: The rounded stage product and Section 4.4 estimate are not a universal inclusion rate.

visuals.fallback: Protocol and inclusion evidence remain separate: 181,857 pages from 200 WARC files in Common Crawl CC-MAIN-2025-51; S1 page prevalence 3.4%; S2 extraction survival 71.9%; S3 curation survival 5.5%; Section 4.4 derived estimate about 0.13%; Introduction disputed value 0.15%.

visuals.title: Controlled preference shifts attenuate after clean SFT
visuals.question: How do effect ranges change across experimental conditions?

items.label: 0.001% poison
items.detail: Tested poison-token rate; this summary does not attach a quantitative effect range to it.

items.label: 0.01% poison
items.detail: Tested poison-token rate; this summary does not attach a quantitative effect range to it.

items.label: Five base-model scales
items.detail: At 0.1% poison across models from 65M to 1.3B parameters. This quantitative value is reported for the 0.1% poison condition.
items.group: Poison rate 0.1% · Base models · domain 17–22 pp

items.label: Post-SFT range
items.detail: Remaining shift after clean supervised fine-tuning at 0.1% poison. This quantitative value is reported for the 0.1% poison condition.
items.group: Poison rate 0.1% · After clean SFT · domain 0–8 pp

items.label: No-label · 709M
items.detail: Remaining no-label-format shift after clean SFT. This quantitative value is reported for the 0.1% poison condition.
items.group: Poison rate 0.1% · No-label after SFT · domain 0–0.5 pp

items.label: No-label · 1.3B
items.detail: Remaining no-label-format shift after clean SFT. This quantitative value is reported for the 0.1% poison condition.
items.group: Poison rate 0.1% · No-label after SFT · domain 0–0.5 pp

visuals.interpretation: The result is a set of condition-specific ranges and points, not a single effect size or a frontier-model claim.
limitations: These controlled synthetic-entity results do not establish the same effect at frontier scale or through a live corpus attack.
visuals.alt_text: The controlled experiment tests poison-token rates of 0.001%, 0.01%, and 0.1%. Quantitative effect ranges shown here apply only to 0.1%: 18.6–20.7 percentage points across five base-model scales and 0.9–7.4 points after clean supervised fine-tuning, with no-label residuals of 0.1 and 0.3 points.
visuals.fallback: The controlled experiment tests poison-token rates of 0.001%, 0.01%, and 0.1%. Quantitative effect ranges shown here apply only to 0.1%: 18.6–20.7 percentage points across five base-model scales and 0.9–7.4 points after clean supervised fine-tuning, with no-label residuals of 0.1 and 0.3 points.

glossary.definition: Adding adversarial examples to data consumed before a model's task-specific or instruction tuning.
The paper's analysis for estimating whether an injected fragment reaches a final training corpus through multiple conditional stages.
A web-archive file format used by Common Crawl to store fetched web content.
The probability that an extracted document remains after heuristic, language, quality, and deduplication filters.
The fraction of pretraining tokens drawn from the controlled poison examples in the model experiment.

revision_notes: Initial evidence-backed explainer fixture for arXiv:2607.15267v1.
The inclusion analysis and controlled model-effect experiment remain explicitly separate.
Exposed the unresolved v1 discrepancy between the Introduction's 0.15% inclusion summary and Section 4.4's stage-derived approximately 0.13% value.
Editorial review and human publication approval remain required.
Visual revision 3: paragraph-scoped figures now encode the manifest-selected topology, conditions, values, and evidence boundaries.
